confidence.disclosure: Route is taken directly from Nasir Khusraw's own Safarnama, one of the most precisely dated medieval travel texts: he records over 125 stops with months and lengths of stay. Wheeler Thackston's 1986 translation and Guy Le Strange's 1893 Syria/Palestine extracts provide the chronology. Dates between 1053 and his death in Yumgan are reconstructed from the Diwan and later Ismaili tradition.

sources: Wheeler M. Thackston (trans.), Naser-e Khosraw's Book of Travels (1986)
Guy Le Strange (trans.), Diary of a Journey Through Syria and Palestine (1893)
Alice C. Hunsberger, Nasir Khusraw, the Ruby of Badakhshan (2000)

journey.event: Born into a family of Khorasan landowner-officials.
Working as a Seljuk revenue official, has the dream-vision that prompts him to abandon his career.
Departs Marv on 5 March 1046 with his brother and a Hindu servant, reaching Nishapur after a few weeks.
Crossed Qumes province, visiting Damghan and the tomb of Bayazid Bistami.
Met the philosopher-physician Ustad Ali Nisai, a pupil of Avicenna.
Passed through Qazvin on the way north to Azerbaijan.
Reached Tabriz on 20 Safar 438 AH (26 August 1046); met the poet Qatran.
Continued northwest through Marand and Khoy in Azerbaijan.
Crossed into Armenia, visiting Van and Akhlat.
Travelled through Bitlis on the way to upper Mesopotamia.
Visited Amid (Diyarbakır), praising its black basalt walls.
Stayed in Aleppo, recording its citadel and markets.
Met the blind Arab philosopher-poet Abu al-Ala al-Maarri, of whom he speaks with deep admiration.
Reached the Syrian coast and recorded the prosperous Fatimid port of Tripoli.
Travelled south along the coast through Beirut.
Described Tyre as a thriving five- and six-storey port renowned among Syrian coastal cities.
Stopped at Acre on the way inland to Jerusalem.
Spent three months in Jerusalem, leaving the most detailed 11th-century Muslim description of the Haram al-Sharif.
First of four pilgrimages to Mecca during his seven-year journey.
Arrived in Fatimid Cairo in August 1047 and remained for three years, training as an Ismaili dai under al-Muayyad fi al-Din al-Shirazi.
Travelled up the Nile from Cairo before crossing to the Red Sea on his way to a later Hajj.
Crossed from Aswan to the Red Sea port of Aydhab to embark for the Hijaz.
Performed his second Hajj, returning thereafter to Cairo.
Performed his third Hajj while serving the Fatimid dawa.
Performed his fourth and final Hajj before turning eastward.
Crossed the Arabian peninsula via the Carmathian-controlled oasis of al-Hasa.
Reached Basra after the Arabian crossing, almost destitute.
Crossed Persia eastward through Isfahan on the return route.
Returned home to Balkh in October 1052, completing the seven-year journey.
Forced into exile in the remote Badakhshan valley of Yumgan, where he wrote his Ismaili philosophical works.
Died in Yumgan; his shrine became a pilgrimage site for Central Asian Ismailis.
